2: new name. Abram's name was changed to Abraham, Jacob's to Israel, and Saul's to Paul-all to signify a new character, a new devotion, a new condition, a new life. So it will be with Israel. The new name given to Israel may be The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Mc Conkie, Millennial Messiah, 131).
4: [so God will "desire" thee, as a husband his wife, and the land will be married to God. This is remarriage to Israel. Recall Christ's first miracle, water into wine at the marriage feast; he wants to marry Israel forever, sealing them to him; Beulah - married wife. The land with her rightful heirs are called married in the covenant, to their God. Eternal marriage, land of inheritance in the covenant, sealed to God, seed forever, land of inheritance forever] All of this is commentary on the law of Moses. Lev. 26:43 - God is really upset that they haven't let the land rest on the sabbath. He mentions it three or four times. (45) But I won't cast them away...I am longsuffering, etc., and they won't be utterly destroyed. I AM THE LORD THEIR GOD. He will remember the covenant. I never forget. (Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes)
The JST replaces "Hephzibah" with "Delightful," and "Beulah" with "Union."

12: cup of iniquity is full. We have received a great many warnings. The great day of the Millennium will come in; the wicked will be consumed and peace and righteousness will dwell upon all the face of the earth for one thousand years. (Joseph Fielding Smith, Church History and Modern Revelation 1:191-92.) `The opening verses of Isa. 63 give two important questions and answers about the second coming of Christ. The first question is, "Who comes with dyed garments, . . . traveling in the greatness of his strength?" The answer: "The Righteous One, who is mighty to save" (63:1; see Rev. 19:13, 15; 14:20; D&C 133:46-48). The second question is, "Why are your garments red, as one who has been treading in the winefat?" The answer: "Because I have trodden the winepress alone, and I will trample the wicked and stain my garments with their blood" (63:2-3). The second coming of the Lord will be a "day of vengeance," but it will result in "the year of my redeemed" (63:4). D&C 133:50-53 quotes Isa. 63:3-9 with a few slight changes. Isa. 63:1-9 and 64:1-5 are both quoted in D&C 133 but in reverse order. (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.553)

63&64 There is also much biblical phraseology in the Doctrine and Covenants; for example, section 133 [D&C 133] is similar in content and language to Isaiah 63 and 64 [Isa. 63-64]. One cannot be familiar with the Doctrine and Covenants and the Bible without being aware that both books support each other, and are tied together at key points by subject matter and phraseology. Indeed, the [page 43] revelations contained in the Doctrine and Covenants are a strong witness for the truth and worth of the Bible. (Robert J. Matthews, "The Bible and Its Role in the Restoration," Ensign, July 1979, 42-43)

Isaiah has pictured this great day when the Lord shall come with his garments, or apparel, red and glorious, to take vengeance on the ungodly. [Isaiah 63:1-6.] This will be a day of mourning to the wicked, but a day of gladness to all who have kept his commandments. Do not let any one think that this is merely figurative language, it is literal, and as surely as we live that day of wrath will come when the
